core_intuitions: A verifiable reward is a deterministic PROGRAM V(q,o)->{0,1}, not a learned network: it IS ground truth for 'got it right', so there is no approximation error to exploit and you can optimize against it arbitrarily hard.

R1-Zero emergence: because longer reasoning correlates with reaching the right answer on hard problems, the group-relative gradient up-weights 'spend more tokens checking/backtracking/verifying' — long CoT and the 'aha moment' are DISCOVERED as the cheapest path to correctness, not demonstrated.

The non-negotiable precondition is a mixed-outcome group: a GRPO group needs BOTH wins and losses. If pass@G ~ 0 (too hard) or ~ 1 (too easy) the reward variance is zero, every advantage is zero, and no gradient flows — the 'dead group'. Emergence only turns in the difficulty sweet spot, hence curriculum.
Verifiers must PARSE -> NORMALIZE -> COMPARE for equivalence, never raw string-match (1/2 = 0.5 = 0.50); a weak verifier's false-negatives poison training by punishing correct answers.

Code is the strongest AND most dangerous verifier: model output is adversarial by construction, so execution must be sandboxed (isolation + rlimits + no net/FS + hidden tests), and a crashed grader must count as reward 0.

Reward hacking is NOT eliminated by RLVR — it MOVES from statistics (a learned RM drifting off-distribution, Goodhart) to software (the policy fuzzing your verifier/sandbox for the cheapest exploit); threat-model the grader like a public API taking untrusted input.

existing.name: rlvr-rm-vs-verifier
existing.issue: Clean two-panel contrast: RLHF learned-reward with a red hack-loop vs RLVR programmatic checker with a straight-through exact path. Reads correctly and legibly in both light and dark themes; the surfaces flip and the 'no hack loop' point lands. Serves the first core intuition well.

existing.name: rlvr-length-emergence-loop
existing.issue: The figure is not fully theme-aware: unlike the other three figures it renders with LIGHT box fills and DARK free-standing text in BOTH themes. In dark mode this leaves the title, the subtitle, the italic footer, the vertical 'length / long / short' axis labels, the 'repeat over thousands of steps' loop label, and the 'few winners, many losers' note as dark-on-dark and effectively invisible; the boxed body text survives only because each box carries its own light fill.
existing.improvement: Adopt the same theme-token pattern the other three chapter figures already use so dark theme flips BOTH the box surfaces and the ink: every free-floating text element (title, subtitle, footer, the vertical length-axis labels, the loop/step annotation, the 'few winners, many losers' note) must resolve to an ink token with adequate contrast on the page background in both themes. While in there, re-check that the bottom green 'STEP N — ... ~70%+ correct' box text and the right-side feedback-loop curve both sit fully inside the viewBox with margin (the bottom-right text currently reads as touching the edge in dark).

existing.name: rlvr-verifier-spectrum
existing.issue: The exact->general axis (symbolic / code-exec / LLM-judge / learned-RM) with the 'least hackable, narrowest' -> 'most hackable, broadest' framing and per-node example tasks is crisp and legible in both themes; the gradient bar and callouts read cleanly. Serves the 'prefer the most exact verifier the task allows' intuition well.

existing.name: rlvr-reasoning-recipe-pipeline
existing.issue: Numbered 0-5 recipe (BASE -> DATA -> REWARD -> RL -> MONITOR -> BANK & BROADEN) is comprehensive, accurate, and readable in both themes, with the final stage visually distinguished as the non-verifiable reintroduction. Good closing synthesis; no invented benchmark numbers. Keep.

new_figures.name: rlvr-dead-group-sweet-spot
new_figures.concept: Why RLVR only learns when the GRPO group has mixed outcomes: all-fail and all-pass groups have zero reward variance -> zero advantage -> no gradient; only the mixed 'sweet spot' produces a learning signal.
new_figures.rationale: This is the chapter's single most-emphasized intuition that currently has NO dedicated figure: it is flagged as 'the one precondition without which it fails', gets a full warning box, is the crux of an Interview Corner answer, and is a Key Takeaway. The length-emergence figure shows a healthy 'few winners, many losers' group but never shows the two FAILURE regimes (dead groups) or the difficulty band, which is exactly where the intuition lives. A single static frame makes the variance->advantage->gradient mechanism click and motivates curriculum.
new_figures.placement: The R1-Zero phenomenon section, immediately after the warning box 'Common pitfall: R1-Zero needs a base model that can sometimes succeed' (right after the {{fig:rlvr-length-emergence-loop}} marker's subsection).
new_figures.brief: A three-column comparison, each column a single GRPO group of G=6 sampled responses on ONE problem, drawn as a row of 6 small chips (use the chapter's existing win/lose visual language: filled 'win' chips vs hollow 'lose' chips, matching rlvr-length-emergence-loop). LEFT column titled 'Too hard (pass@G ~ 0)': all 6 chips are losses; below it show R = {0,0,0,0,0,0}, mean = 0, and a small formula strip 'A_i = (R_i - mean)/sigma = 0 for all i' with sigma = 0; stamp it 'DEAD GROUP - no gradient'. RIGHT column titled 'Too easy (pass@G ~ 1)': all 6 chips are wins; R = {1,1,1,1,1,1}, mean = 1, again every deviation 0, 'A_i = 0'; stamp 'DEAD GROUP - no gradient'. CENTER column, visually emphasized (thicker border / soft highlight) titled 'Sweet spot (pass@G mixed)': 2 win chips + 4 lose chips; R = {1,1,0,0,0,0}, mean = 1/3, show the winners carrying a positive advantage arrow (up) and losers a negative advantage arrow (down), with a strip 'variance > 0  ->  advantage != 0  ->  gradient flows'; stamp 'LIVE - reasoning behaviors get selected'. Beneath all three, a horizontal difficulty axis running 'easy (left mislabel avoided: keep hard on the right)'—label ends 'pass@G = 1 (too easy)' on one end and 'pass@G = 0 (too hard)' on the other, with a highlighted middle band labeled 'learnable band ~ mixed outcomes' aligned under the center column; add a short curriculum note: 'curriculum: keep prompts in the mixed band; ramp difficulty as the policy improves'. Constraints: use ONLY the small illustrative integers shown (G=6, 2 wins) and the exact fractions they imply (mean 0, 1/3, 1) — invent NO benchmark or percentage numbers beyond the qualitative pass@G ~ 0 / ~ 1 tags; keep advantage expressed symbolically (A_i = (R_i - mean)/sigma), no fabricated numeric advantages needed. Must be fully legible as a static frame and theme-safe (use win/lose and positive/negative semantic tokens, no hardcoded page-dependent grays for free text).

new_figures.name: rlvr-hacking-moved-statistics-to-software
new_figures.concept: RLVR does not remove reward hacking — it converts STATISTICAL hacking (a learned RM drifting off-distribution) into SOFTWARE hacking (the policy fuzzing your verifier/sandbox), so defenses become engineering, not statistics.
new_figures.rationale: The 'it's not gone, it moved' mental model is a whole chapter section plus an Interview Corner plus a Key Takeaway, and it directly nuances the earlier rm-vs-verifier figure's 'no hack loop' claim — yet it has no figure. This is the chapter's most memorable and most interview-tested conceptual reframe, and a side-by-side static contrast (drift landscape vs adversarial-API-under-fuzzing) makes the shift concrete and pairs each concrete exploit with its concrete defense.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Reward hacking in RLVR: it's not gone, it moved', after the bulleted list of observed hacks and near the closing 'The mental model: RLVR converts statistical reward-hacking into software security.' paragraph.
new_figures.brief: A two-panel contrast joined by a bold center arrow labeled 'the hacking MOVES — it does not disappear'. LEFT panel titled 'Statistical hacking (learned reward model)': draw a simple 1-D reward-vs-input-region sketch with two curves — 'true quality' and 'learned reward r_phi' — that track together on-distribution but DIVERGE off-distribution; place a policy marker that has drifted to the peak of r_phi where true-quality is low, annotated 'Goodhart: optimize the proxy, true quality falls'; caption 'defense = statistics (KL anchor, early stop, RM ensembles, stay near data)'. RIGHT panel titled 'Software hacking (verifier / sandbox)': draw the verifier+sandbox as a labeled API box ('V(q,o) / code sandbox'); draw the policy as a 'fuzzer' firing several exploit arrows AT the box, each arrow labeled with a concrete observed hack and paired with a compact defense tag — (1) 'hard-code leaked test outputs' -> 'hide + randomize tests, no FS/net'; (2) 'spam many \boxed{} candidates / regex trick' -> 'take LAST boxed only, fuzz the extractor'; (3) 'fork-bomb / crash the grader for a default pass' -> 'real isolation + rlimits; crash = reward 0'; (4) 'farm oversized format / length bonus' -> 'keep shaping small AND contingent on attempting'. Across the bottom, one takeaway banner: 'RLVR converts statistical drift into software security — threat-model your grader like a public API taking untrusted input.' Constraints: no benchmark numbers; the reward-landscape curves are schematic (unlabeled axes with only 'on-distribution' / 'off-distribution' regions), not plotted data; keep the four exploit/defense pairs readable with no overlap; theme-safe tokens for all text and the two curves so both panels read in light and dark; must work fully as a static frame.

summary: The chapter is already well-served by four figures covering the rm-vs-verifier contrast, the length-emergence loop, the verifier spectrum, and the full recipe pipeline; three are keeps and render cleanly in both themes. One (rlvr-length-emergence-loop) needs a refine: it is not theme-aware like its siblings, so in dark mode its free-standing title/subtitle/footer/axis/loop text goes dark-on-dark and disappears. Two concept gaps justify new static figures: (1) the dead-group / mixed-outcome precondition — the chapter's most-emphasized intuition (warning box + interview corner + key takeaway) with no figure, showing all-fail and all-pass groups as zero-variance dead groups vs the mixed sweet spot that produces gradient, plus the learnable difficulty band; and (2) the 'reward hacking moved from statistics to software' mental model — a full section + interview corner + key takeaway, contrasting off-distribution RM drift with the policy fuzzing an adversarial verifier/sandbox API, pairing each concrete exploit with its concrete defense. Both are crisp static infographics; no long-animation is warranted.
